name: Utilities & Resilience
description: Centralized error handling, retry logic with exponential backoff, Zod-based input validation, and rate limiting for production resilience.

intent.what: Provides typed error classes, retry mechanisms, Zod schema-based validation, and rate limiting to ensure system resilience and data integrity.
intent.why: Enables graceful failure handling, automatic recovery from transient errors, TypeScript-first input sanitization via Zod, and protection against abuse through rate limiting.

inputs.name: error
inputs.description: Raw error from Supabase or other sources

inputs.name: schema
inputs.description: Zod schema to validate data against

inputs.name: config
inputs.description: Configuration for retry or rate limiting

outputs.description: Normalized error, retry result, validation result, or rate limit status

steps: Use handleSupabaseError() to normalize any error into typed SupabaseError
Use withRetry() for operations that may fail transiently
Use withTimeout() for operations that must complete within time limit
Create Zod schemas with z.object(), z.string(), z.number(), etc.
Use validateInput() or validateOrFail() with Zod schemas for validation
Use rateLimit() or createRateLimiter() for abuse protection

logic.summary: Utilities provide foundational resilience mechanisms. Error handling normalizes all errors into typed classes for consistent handling. Retry logic uses configurable backoff strategies (linear, exponential, fixed) for transient failures. Validation uses Zod (v4) for TypeScript-first schema validation with automatic type inference. Rate limiting protects against abuse through sliding window counters.

flow.detail: handleSupabaseError() inspects error structure and returns appropriate typed error
withRetry() wraps function, retries on transient errors with configurable backoff
withTimeout() creates AbortController, rejects if promise exceeds timeout
validateInput() uses Zod schema.safeParse() to validate data and return structured errors
rateLimit() checks counter for identifier, allows or denies based on window
Interval clears expired entries to prevent memory leaks

rules: Default retry count is 2 with exponential backoff starting at 1000ms
Rate limit windows are in milliseconds; default is 60000ms (1 minute)
All validation uses Zod schemas for TypeScript-first type safety
All errors must be normalized through handleSupabaseError() before user-facing handling
Rate limiters must be disposed when no longer needed to clear intervals
anti_patterns: Do not retry non-idempotent mutations without understanding consequences
Do not skip validation for user input
Do not use rate limiting for internal service-to-service calls
Do not catch errors without normalizing them first
Do not create rate limiters without disposing them when done
Do not use fixed backoff for network errors (prefer exponential)
Do not use custom validation when Zod schemas can handle it

functions.name: handleSupabaseError
functions.description: Normalizes any error into typed SupabaseError

functions.name: withRetry
functions.description: Retries function with configurable backoff strategy

functions.name: withTimeout
functions.signature: <T>(promise: Promise<T>, timeoutMs: number, message?: string) => Promise<T>

functions.name: validateInput
functions.description: Validates data against Zod schema, returns { valid, errors, data }

functions.name: validateOrFail
functions.description: Validates data against Zod schema, throws ValidationError if invalid

functions.name: createValidator
functions.description: Creates reusable validator with parse, safeParse, validate methods

functions.name: rateLimit
functions.signature: (identifier: string, config?: RateLimitConfig) => Promise<{ allowed: boolean; remaining: number; resetAt: number }>
functions.description: Quick rate limit check using default limiter

when_to_use: When handling errors from Supabase operations
When implementing retry logic for transient failures
When validating user input before database operations using Zod schemas
When protecting endpoints from abuse with rate limiting
When enforcing timeouts for long-running operations
when_not_to_use: When errors are already typed and handled specifically
When validation is handled by database constraints alone
When rate limiting is handled at infrastructure level (CDN, API gateway)
When retry logic is not needed for idempotent operations
reasoning_steps: Identify error source and determine appropriate error type
Choose retry strategy based on error type (exponential for network, fixed for rate limits)
Define Zod schema matching expected data structure with appropriate constraints
Configure rate limits based on endpoint sensitivity
Always normalize errors before user-facing handling
Use type guards for specific error handling in catch blocks
Leverage Zod's type inference for automatic TypeScript types
